Model predictive control (MPC) with learned world models has emerged as a promising paradigm for
embodied control, particularly for its ability to generalize zero-shot when deployed in new environments.
However, learned world models often struggle with long-horizon control due to the accumulation of
prediction errors and the exponentially growing search space. In this work, we address these challenges
by learning latent world models at multiple temporal scales and performing hierarchical planning across
these scales, enabling long-horizon reasoning while substantially reducing inference-time planning
complexity. Our approach serves as a plug-in planning abstraction that applies across diverse latent
world-model architectures and domains. We demonstrate that this hierarchical approach enables
zero-shot control on real-world non-greedy robotic tasks, achieving a 70% success rate on pick-&-place
using only a final goal specification, compared to 0% for a single-level world model. In addition, across
physics-based simulated environments including push manipulation and maze navigation, hierarchical
planning achieves higher success while requiring up to 3× less planning-time compute.

1 Introduction
Planning with learned world models has emerged as a powerful paradigm for embodied decision making.

windows.label: Human Written
windows.text:
By learning predictive models of environment dynamics from experience and using them to simulate future
outcomes, world models enable agents to reason about the long-term consequences of actions (Sutton, 1981;
Deisenroth and Rasmussen, 2011). Recent advances in representation learning and latent-space modeling
have improved the scalability of this approach, making it possible to train world models directly from high-
dimensional observations such as images and videos (Ha and Schmidhuber, 2018; Hafner et al., 2023; Hansen
et al., 2023). More recently, self-supervised learning has enabled training world models from large collections
of unlabeled, task-agnostic offline trajectories, allowing zero-shot deployment in downstream robotic control
and navigation (Assran et al., 2025; Sobal et al., 2025; Bar et al., 2025; Zhou et al., 2024; Terver et al.,
2026b). Despite this progress, learned world models continue to struggle with long-horizon control due to two
fundamental challenges: (i) prediction errors compound over time, leading to increasingly inaccurate rollouts
(Talvitie, 2014; Janner et al., 2019), and (ii) even under a perfect model, planning becomes computationally
expensive as the search space grows exponentially with horizon (Ichter et al., 2020).
A classic approach to long-horizon control is to introduce hierarchy into the decision-making structure. By
reasoning at multiple temporal scales, agents can plan coarsely over long horizons while retaining fine-grained
control at short horizons. This intuition underlies a large body of work on temporal abstraction in reinforcement
learning, including options, skills, and hierarchical policies (Sutton et al., 1999; Bacon et al., 2017; Hafner et al.,
2022; Park et al., 2023), as well as hierarchical world models that capture dynamics at multiple timescales
(Gumbsch et al., 2023; Chitnis et al., 2024; Schiewer et al., 2024; Hansen et al.,

windows.label: Human Written
windows.text:
2024). In parallel, the optimal
control literature has explored hierarchical model predictive control (MPC) for multi-task settings (Fang et al.,
2019; Li et al., 2021; Kögel et al., 2025). However, these approaches remain limited in complementary ways.
Reinforcement learning methods typically rely on task-specific rewards, which restrict their applicability in
zero-shot settings; even goal-conditioned and zero-shot RL methods (Park et al., 2023, 2024b) still struggle

to generalize to environments and tasks beyond the training distribution (Sobal et al., 2025). Conversely,
existing hierarchical MPC methods are not task-specific but are largely restricted to low-dimensional state
spaces, hand-engineered representations, or known dynamics, limiting their applicability to high-dimensional
observation spaces such as raw pixels. As a result, it remains unclear how to realize hierarchical planning for
zero-shot control directly on learned world models from high-dimensional observations.
In this work, we propose Hierarchical Planning with Latent World Models (HWM), a framework for zero-shot
hierarchical model predictive control using learned latent world models. Rather than learning hierarchical
policies, HWM introduces temporal hierarchy directly at inference time by planning over multiple timescales
on task-agnostic world models trained via next-latent prediction. Crucially, our approach is defined by how
the hierarchical levels interact. HWM learns world models operating at different temporal resolutions within a
shared latent space, enabling a direct coupling between levels: high-level predictions act as subgoals for low-level
MPC via latent-state matching. This removes the need for inverse models, skill learning, or goal-conditioned
policies, and allows hierarchical planning to operate entirely on pretrained world models. To support efficient
long-horizon planning, we further introduce a learned action encoder that compresses sequences of primitive
actions between waypoint states into latent macro-actions. This reduces the dimensionality of the high-level
search space and makes planning more tractable. By decoupling long-horizon reasoning from fine-grained
control, HWM mitigates error accumulation and reduces overall planning complexity.

We summarize our contributions as follows:
• We introduce HWM, a hierarchical MPC framework that couples multiple learned world models via a
shared latent space, enabling direct subgoal transfer across levels without hierarchical policies, skill learning,
or task-specific rewards.
• We show that HWM unlocks a new capability for zero-shot world-model planning: solving non-greedy
real-robot tasks from visual inputs, where success requires temporarily moving away from the goal. HWM
achieves 70% success on Franka pick-&-place from a single goal image, compared to 0% for VJEPA2-AC,
and also outperforms strong vision-language-action models trained on ∼ 77× more robotic interaction data.
• We demonstrate that HWM is a modular, model-agnostic planning abstraction, consistently improving
zero-shot performance across diverse latent world models, including VJEPA2-AC (robot manipulation),
DINO-WM (push manipulation), and PLDM (maze navigation).

windows.label: Human Written
windows.text:
• We show that hierarchical planning improves both performance and efficiency on long-horizon tasks,
achieving substantial success-rate gains (up to +44% and +39%) while reducing inference-time planning
cost by up to 4× compared to single-level planners.

2.3 Top-Down Hierarchical Planning
Latent world models naturally support hierarchical planning, as different levels of abstraction can reason over
different temporal horizons within a shared predictive framework. By planning at an appropriate temporal
resolution, hierarchical methods avoid direct optimization over long sequences of primitive actions while
retaining fine-grained control.
In this work, we adopt a top-down hierarchical planning strategy that operates entirely in a learned latent
space (Fig. 2). At a high level, the agent plans abstract latent actions toward a final goal using a long-horizon
world model. The resulting predicted latent states serve as intermediate subgoals. A low-level planner then
optimizes short-horizon sequences of primitive actions to reach the next subgoal, repeating this process in a
receding-horizon MPC manner.

Empirical Evaluation of Hierarchical Planning Across World Models and Tasks
We evaluate whether hierarchical planning (HWM) enables zero-shot long-horizon control in regimes where
planning with a single-level learned world model fails. We apply HWM to three representative latent world-
model architectures—VJEPA2-AC (Assran et al., 2025), PLDM (Sobal et al., 2025), and DINO-WM (Zhou
et al., 2024)—and, for each, consider a benchmark domain from the original work while substantially increasing
task horizon and complexity. For VJEPA2-AC, we study real-world pick-&-place and drawer manipulation
on a Franka robot arm without explicit subgoals. For DINO-WM, we consider the Push-T task with task
horizons extended from 25 to 75 timesteps. For PLDM, we evaluate point-maze navigation with train–test
layout mismatch while quadrupling map size. We summarize our main findings as follows:
1. 
Zero-shot non-greedy control: Pick-&-place success improves from 0% to 70% on Franka with VJEPA2-AC.
2. Long-horizon reasoning: Push-T success increases from 17% to 61% with DINO-WM.
3. OOD generalization: HWM improves PLDM performance on navigation in larger, unseen mazes.
4. Compute efficiency: Comparable or better planning relative to single-level planners with 3× less compute.
3.1 Franka Arm with Robotiq Gripper
We evaluate HWM on real-world multi-stage manipulation with a Franka arm equipped with a Robotiq
gripper, resembling the Droid setup. We run pick-&-place and drawer opening/closin

ng. These tasks require
non-greedy behavior, where successful execution depends on intermediate motions that temporarily increase
distance to the goal—a regime in which single-level planners typically fail.

and drawer manipulation using a 7-DoF Franka Emika Panda arm with a two-finger gripper. Pick-&-place

Baselines. VJEPA2-AC (Assran et al., 2025) is a single-level, world-model-based planner. Because we use
the same architecture and training data for our low-level world model, this comparison isolates the effect of
hierarchical planning. We additionally evaluate three vision–language-action models (VLA) baselines Octo,

Data. World models are trained on approximately 130 hours of unlabeled real-robot manipulation data from
DROID (Khazatsky et al., 2024) and RoboSet (Kumar et al., 2023). Observations include RGB images and
end-effector proprioception; actions correspond to end-effector delta poses (see appendix B.2 for details).
Hierarchical Planning Setup. The agent is provided with the current and goal observations, each specified by
RGB images and end-effector pose

windows.label: Human Written
windows.text:
es. Agent’s actions are end-effector delta poses that are mapped to joints
delta poses via inverse kinematics. For each action, the corresponding joints delta is executed in a blocking
fashion. The high-level planner optimizes over latent actions of dimension 4, using 3000 CEM samples with a
prediction horizon of 2, while the low-level planner uses 800 CEM samples with a prediction horizon of 2. The
agent replans every k = 1 step in standard MPC manner. Planning rollouts are executed in parallel across
GPUs, making the approach computationally efficient even with large numbers of trajectory samples. Full
CEM hyperparameters are provided in appendix C.
World Model Architectures. We follow the VJEPA2-AC training recipe for the low-level world model (see
appendix B.2). To train the high-level world model, We use N = 3 waypoint states sampled from trajectory
segments spanning up to 4 seconds, with the middle waypoint chosen uniformly at random. Latent actions are
encoded from the intervening low-level actions using a transformer-based action encoder, with the CLS token
projected to the latent action space. For visualization, we decode latent representations into RGB images
using a ViT decoder; these reconstructions are used solely for qualitative analysis and are not involved in
planning or training (details in appendix E).

Takeaway: HWM enables zero-shot, non-greedy manipulation on a real robot from a single goal image,
where flat world-model planners fail.
3.2 Push-T
We instantiate HWM on top of DINO-WM (Zhou et al., 2024) and evaluate it on the Push-T environment
introduced by Chi et al.

windows.label: Human Written
windows.text:
. (2025) and adapted by DINO-WM to a goal-conditioned setting. The task requires
fine-grained control to push a T-shaped object to match a goal configuration sampled from a validation
trajectory. To stress-test long-horizon planning, we consider extended-horizon settings with start–goal pairs
separated by up to d = 75 timesteps, exceeding the d = 25 timesteps used in the original DINO-WM evaluation.
All models are trained on the offline Push-T dataset released with DINO-WM (details in appendix B.3).
Baselines. We compare against (i) DINO-WM, (ii) offline goal-conditioned RL methods GCIQL and HIQL
(Kostrikov et al., 2021; Park et al., 2023), (iii) HILP (Park et al., 2024b), a zero-shot unsupervised RL method.

4 Analysis of Hierarchical Planning
We conduct ablation experiments to analyze factors that influence hierarchical planning performance. All
models in this section use a VJEPA2 backbone trained on real-world robotic datasets (training details in
section 3.1).
4.1 Do latent actions help with high level planning?
We examine whether training the high-level world model benefits from encoding the full low-level action
subsequence between waypoint states, as opposed to representing high-level actions solely as the net end-
effector displacement between waypoints. We do not consider using raw action sequences directly, as this
would resemble flat planning and would not reduce the action search space for planning.

displacement, discarding information critical for predicting feasible transitions, whereas a learned latent action
can compactly encode the structure of the full action sequence.

5 Related Works
World Models and Plannin

windows.label: Human Written
windows.text:
ng. Learning predictive models of environment dynamics is a core idea in model-based
reinforcement learning (Sutton, 1981), with early work focusing on state-space dynamics for planning and
policy optimization (Deisenroth and Rasmussen, 2011; Sutton, 1991). More recent approaches learn dynamics
from high-dimensional observations, either directly in pixel space or in a learned latent space (Ebert et al.,
2017; Ha and Schmidhuber, 2018; Hafner et al., 2019; Schwarzer et al., 2020; Hafner et al., 2023; Watter et al.,
2015; Nair et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022; Bagatella et al., 2026), enabling efficient learning and planning
from pixels in both simulation and real-world robotics.
Recent work further demonstrates that task-agnostic generative world models trained on large-scale, real-world
video can produce realistic and diverse simulations of physical environments (Hu et al., 2023; Yang et al.,
2023; Bruce et al., 2024; Bai et al., 2025), highlighting the potential of scaling predictive modeling beyond
task-specific datasets. Closely related to our setting, a line of work on latent world models shows that models
trained on reward-free offline data via next-latent prediction can generalize zero-shot to downstream control
tasks. These approaches leverage the learned dynamics to simulate action outcomes and select actions through
cost-based optimization, without requiring task-specific policy training (Assran et al., 2025; Sobal et al., 2025;
Terver et al., 2026b; Zhou et al., 2024).
Limitations of Long Horizon Planning. Learned world models remain brittle when used for long-horizon planning
due to compounding prediction errors (Talvitie, 2014; Janner et al., 2019). Moreover, long-horizon planning is
challenging even under a perfect model because the search tree grows exponentially with the branching factor
10

windows.label: Human Written
windows.text: and horizon (a manifestation of the curse of horizon) (Ichter et al

windows.label: Human Written
windows.text:
l., 2020).
Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning. To address long-horizon control, prior work has explored hierarchical
reinforcement learning through temporal abstraction via options and skills (Sutton et al., 1999; Bacon et al.,
2017; Hafner et al., 2022). In parallel, many model-based RL approaches incorporate hierarchical world models
to capture dynamics at multiple temporal resolutions (Gumbsch et al., 2023; Chitnis et al., 2024; Schiewer
et al., 2024; Hansen et al., 2024). While these methods enable improved long-horizon reasoning, they typically
rely on task-specific policy learning, which limits their applicability to zero-shot and general-purpose control.
Hierarchical Model Predictive Control. In the optimal control literature, hierarchical model predictive control
(MPC) has been explored for long-horizon, multi-task settings (Fang et al., 2019; Li et al., 2021; Kögel
et al., 2025). However, existing hierarchical MPC approaches are largely restricted to low-dimensional state
spaces, hand-engineered representations, or known dynamics, limiting their applicability to high-dimensional
observation spaces such as raw pixels.
We provide a more detailed comparison of these approaches, including their relationship to our method and a
summary across key design axes, in appendix A.
6 Conclusion
We presented HWM, a hierarchical planning framework that performs model predictive control over learned
latent dynamics at multiple temporal scales. By decoupling long-horizon reasoning from fine-grained action
execution, HWM mitigates compounding prediction errors and the exponentially growing search spaces
that limit single-level world-model-based planners. Across diverse world-model architectures, hierarchical
planning consistently outperforms flat planners on non-greedy, long-horizon tasks while substantially improving
inference-time efficiency.
Despite these gains, long-horizon manipulation remains challenging, and performance degrades for all methods
as horizons increas

windows.label: Human Written
windows.text:
se. Addressing this limitation will likely require advances in representation learning, including
more abstract representations for higher-level world models; uncertainty-aware planning to better handle the
increased stochasticity and model uncertainty inherent to long-horizon real-world prediction; and improved
hierarchical planning algorithms that allow feedback and interaction across levels of the hierarchy instead
of strictly top-down decomposition. We view HWM as a step toward scalable, general-purpose planning
with learned world models, and hope this work encourages future research on hierarchical abstractions for
long-horizon decision making in complex environments.

A Relationship to Prior Hierarchical Model-Based Methods
In this section, we clarify the distinctions between HWM and prior methods along key axes.
We compare methods along the following dimensions:
• Observation space: whether planning operates on structured state or learned representations from
high-dimensional observations (e.g., pixels).
• Training paradigm: whether hierarchy is learned via policies/skills or introduced at inference time on
pretrained models.
• Hierarchical interface: how high-level decisions are translated into low-level control.

they support zero-shot planning, the observation space of the model, the training paradigm, and the hierarchical
interface between levels. We denote “partial” zero-shot when a method generalizes within a fixed, reward-defined task
distribution but remains fundamentally tied to reward supervision, and thus does not support task-agnostic planning.
In contrast, HWM enables fully reward-free, task-agnostic zero-shot planning.
Table 5 highlights several important distinctions between HWM and prior hierarchical model-based approaches.
Hierarchical Model Predictive Control methods (e.g., CAVIN, LAT, Safe HMPC) typically operate in a zero-shot
setting, but are largely restricted to low-dimensional or structured state representations, often with known or
simplified dynamics. This makes it challenging to directly apply them to high-dimensional perceptual inputs.
Hierarchical Model-Based Reinforcement Learning methods (e.g., Director, Puppeteer, IQL-TD-MPC) address
this limitation by learning dynamics in latent representation spaces derived from high-dimensional observations.
However, these approaches rely on reward supervision and are typically trained for specific task objectives,
which can limit their applicability in zero-shot settings.
In contrast, HWM operates on learned latent world models trained purely via next-latent prediction from
high-dimensional observations, without reward supervisio

planning is performed directly in the learned latent space. Concretely, both levels of the hierarchy optimize a
shared objective that minimizes the distance between predicted latent states and a goal latent representation,
with high-level predictions serving as subgoals for the low-level planner (section 2.3). This allows the system
to compose behaviors at test time and adapt to new goals and environments in a fully zero-shot manner.

Among prior work, THICK is most closely related to our setting, as it also learns hierarchical latent world
models from pixel observations. However, it is developed within a reward-predictive model-based RL framework,
where models are trained with reward signals and optimized for reward-based objectives. As a result, its
generalization is primarily evaluated within a fixed task distribution. In addition, its MPC-based evaluation
focuses on relatively simple simulated settings with aligned training and test distributions and fixed goals.

C Planning Details
For Franka and Push-T, we use the Cross-Entropy Method (CEM) (Rubinstein and Kroese, 2004) for trajectory
optimization in continuous action spaces. At each planning step, CEM maintains a factorized Gaussian over
action sequences, iteratively sampling trajectories, evaluating them with the world model, and refitting to
elite samples by updating the mean and variance. To improve stability and avoid premature collapse, variance
updates are smoothed with an exponential moving average. After a fixed number of iterations, the optimized
mean action sequence is executed, and planning proceeds in a receding-horizon manner. See table 9 and
table 10 for the single-level and hierarchical planner parameters, respectively.
For Diverse Maze, we use Model Predictive Path Integral (MPPI) (Williams et al., 2017) for planning. MPPI
updates a nominal action sequence by importance-weighting sampled trajectories using exponential costs;
unlike CEM, it applies soft weights over all samples, yielding smoother updates without hard elite selection.
We use the MPPI implementation from PLDM

J.2 Goal-Conditioned & Zero-Shot RL Methods
GCIQL (Park et al., 2023) – goal-conditioned version of Implicit Q-Learning (Kostrikov et al., 2021), a strong
and widely-used method for offline RL.
HIQL (Park et al., 2023) – a hierarchical GCRL method which trains two policies: one to generate subgoals,
and another one to reach the subgoals. Notably, both policies use the same value function.
HILP (Park et al., 2024b) – a method that learns state representations from the offline data such that
the distance in the learned representation space is proportional to the number of steps between two states.
A direction-conditioned policy is then learned to be able to move along any specified direction in the latent space.
For GCIQL and HIQL, we use the implementations from the repository
2 
of OGBench (Park et al., 2024a).
Likewise, for HILP we use the official implementation
